one_line_takeaway: VIAT recasts adversarial camera viewpoints as the threat and, via a Gaussian-mixture viewpoint attack (GMVFool) driving distribution-based adversarial training, lifts worst-case-viewpoint accuracy from single digits to ~60-80% at no clean-accuracy cost, with a randomized-smoothing certificate (ViewRS) and two new datasets to back it up.
key_finding: VIAT roughly quadruples-to-septuples worst-case-viewpoint accuracy without hurting clean accuracy. On the authors' Table 2, standard ResNet-50 drops to 8.28% under GMVFool viewpoints but VIAT(GMVFool) reaches 59.61% (clean ImageNet unchanged, 85.60%->85.70%); ViT-B/16 goes 29.10%->83.13%. Using GMVFool rather than ViewFool as the inner attack yields both higher robustness and a smaller cross-attack gap, evidencing better generalization from the mixture's viewpoint diversity. GMVFool is ~160x faster than ViewFool (3 min vs 8 hours per object on a V100) while keeping high attack success (>~91% on ResNet-50) and raising distribution entropy sharply as K grows (K=5 entropy -0.69 vs ViewFool -10.14). On the ImageNet-V+ benchmark all 40 pretrained models collapse under adversarial viewpoints (best baseline MAE ViT-H only 29.37%); VIAT lifts every retrained model, with Swin-B rising 24.00%->79.25%. Large VLMs are also brittle to viewpoint OOD despite strong l_p/OOD reputations (CLIP-ResNet50 drops 62.61% on ImageNet-V+; larger models degrade less, BLIP-2 ViT-G only -19.48%). ViewRS certificates confirm the empirical gains theoretically (e.g., ResNet-50 ACR 0.105->0.150, CA 41%->77%).
core_contribution: This paper (an extended journal version of the authors' ICCV work) introduces Viewpoint-Invariant Adversarial Training (VIAT), the first adversarial-training framework aimed at making image classifiers robust to worst-case 3D camera viewpoints rather than to l_p pixel perturbations. It is formulated as a distribution-based minimax problem: the inner maximization learns a distribution of adversarial viewpoints, and the outer minimization trains the classifier on rendered adversarial-viewpoint images plus clean samples. The paper's own novel components are (1) GMVFool, an inner-maximization viewpoint attack that learns a K-component Gaussian mixture distribution over 6-DoF viewpoint parameters (rotation+translation) via NES-estimated gradients and a reparameterization trick, capturing multiple local maxima of the loss landscape; (2) a stochastic-update strategy (only a random subset of objects per class is re-optimized each epoch) and a same-category distribution-sharing strategy to make training tractable and avoid catastrophic overfitting; (3) ViewRS, a certified viewpoint-robustness method that specializes DeformRS-PAR/Randomized Smoothing to the viewpoint-parameter domain using a NeRF renderer in place of an interpolation function; (4) two datasets: IM3D (1K synthetic PBR 3D objects over 100 ImageNet classes, used for training) and ImageNet-V+ (a ~100K-image OOD benchmark of adversarial viewpoints, 10x larger than the prior ImageNet-V). Distinct from the cited prior work ViewFool (Dong et al., a co-author's earlier attack that uses a single unimodal Gaussian and only attacks models), VIAT/GMVFool aim to defend, generate diverse multi-modal adversarial viewpoints, and generalize to unseen objects.
threat_model: The adversary is the choice of 3D camera viewpoint: given a NeRF/Instant-NGP reconstruction of an object, an attacker searches the 6-DoF viewpoint parameter space (camera yaw/pitch/roll psi,theta,phi and translations dx,dy,dz, bounded to a fixed range) to find rendered views that cause a classifier to misclassify. GMVFool operates black-box, needing only query access to the model (NES estimates the classification-loss gradient) and multi-view images of the object (no explicit 3D CAD/mesh). This is a natural/physical robustness threat rather than a digital pixel-perturbation threat: the motivation is safety-critical settings (autonomous driving, robotics, surveillance) where uncommon-but-benign viewpoints already fool models. VIAT is the defense that minimizes expected loss over the worst-case adversarial-viewpoint distribution; ViewRS provides a probabilistic (l2-sense) certificate that predictions stay constant within a certified viewpoint-perturbation radius.
limitations: Requires per-object multi-view images and a NeRF/Instant-NGP reconstruction, so it does not apply to arbitrary single images and inherits a reality gap; training and the benchmark rest on synthetic IM3D objects (100 ImageNet classes only), and real-world validation is limited to 5 Objectron categories and a few recorded videos. GMVFool is a black-box query attack using NES gradient estimates, and results depend on tuned hyperparameters (K=15, pi=0.5, lambda=0.01) whose optimal values differ for attacking vs. defending. The certification is probabilistic and in the l2 sense with a small radius (0.1 corresponds to only about +/-3 deg yaw / +/-18 deg pitch etc.), and ViewRS transfers DeformRS-PAR/Randomized-Smoothing corollaries rather than proving new bounds. Gains are uneven (much larger for transformers than CNNs), and clean-accuracy neutrality is shown only for the two main backbones.

datasets_used: IM3D (paper's own: 1K synthetic PBR 3D objects, 100 ImageNet classes, 10 objects/class, spherical multi-view + camera poses, 9:1 train/val)
ImageNet-V+ (paper's own benchmark: ~100K adversarial-viewpoint images from GMVFool vs ViT-B/16, 10x larger than ImageNet-V)
ImageNet subset (100 categories matching IM3D, for clean training/eval)
Objectron (real-world object-centric videos, 5 categories, for real-world transfer)
MIRO, OOWL, CO3D (other multi-view datasets for eval)
quantitative_results: ResNet-50 accuracy under GMVFool viewpoints: 8.28% standard-trained -> 59.61% VIAT(GMVFool); clean ImageNet 85.60% -> 85.70% (Table 2)
ViT-B/16 under GMVFool: 29.10% -> 83.13%; under ViewFool: 25.70% -> 82.81%; clean 92.88% -> 92.56% (Table 2)
Reported robustness gains vs standard: ResNet-50 +50.63% (GMVFool) / +51.56% (ViewFool); ViT-B/16 +54.03% / +57.11%
GMVFool efficiency: ~3 minutes vs ViewFool ~8 hours per object on one V100 = ~160x faster
Attack success rate on ResNet-50: Random Search 64.12%, ViewFool 91.50%, GMVFool K=1 92.13%, K=5 91.56%; entropy K=5 -0.69 vs ViewFool -10.14 (Table 5)
Best off-the-shelf model on ImageNet-V+ is MAE ViT-H at only 29.37%
VLM zero-shot drop on ImageNet-V+ vs ImageNet: CLIP-ResNet50 -62.61% (65.12%->2.51%), CLIP ViT-L/14 -33.47%, BLIP-2 ViT-G -19.48% (77.40%->57.92%) (Table 7)
ViewRS certification: ACR ResNet-50 0.105->0.150, ViT-B/16 0.167->0.201; CA ResNet-50 41%->77%, EfficientNet-B0 24%->66%, Swin-B 85%->90% (Table 8)
Real-world Objectron accuracy (Standard->VIAT ResNet-50): mug 97.67%->99.07%, camera 81.42%->86.93%, computer 60.19%->63.40% (Table 3)
Chosen hyperparameters: K=15, pi=0.5, lambda=0.01; VIAT fine-tunes 60 epochs at 1:32 adversarial:clean ratio
baselines_compared: VIAT using ViewFool as inner maximization (ablation of the attack)
ViewFool (prior unimodal-Gaussian viewpoint attack) and Random Search as attack baselines

40 pretrained architectures on ImageNet-V+ (VGG, ResNet, Inception, DenseNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet-v2, ViT, DeiT, Swin, MLP-Mixer, adversarially-trained ResNet eps 1/3/5, MAE ViT-B/L/H, AugMix, DeepAugment)
Large VLMs zero-shot: ALBEF, CLIP (RN50/ViT-B/L variants), BLIP, BLIP-2
